name: TitleMax Title Loans
description_short: TitleMax Title Loans Pasadena, Texas — TitleMax is one of the largest vehicle title lenders in the US, offering short-term loans secured by car or motor...
description_long:
TitleMax Title Loans was founded on September 1, 1998, in Columbus, Georgia, and has grown into one of the largest consumer title lending companies in the United States. Owned by privately held TMX Finance LLC (formerly TitleMax Holdings, renamed in 2010), the company operates over 900 stores across 14+ states including Texas, Georgia, Florida, Arizona, Tennessee, and others, with dual corporate offices in Dallas, TX and Savannah, GA. TitleMax holds no CDFI, HUD-approved, NFCC, or similar nonprofit certifications, and is not BBB accredited at the corporate level.

TitleMax's core product is the vehicle title loan — a short-term, high-interest loan secured by the borrower's car or motorcycle title. Borrowers retain use of their vehicle while repaying the loan, with amounts up to approximately $10,000 depending on the state and vehicle value. In Georgia, these are offered as title pawns, which function similarly. TitleMax also offers unsecured personal loans up to $2,500 in Arizona, South Carolina, and Texas. Applications can be completed in-store or initiated online via the proprietary Fast Track Appraiser™ smartphone app, which uses the phone's camera, microphone, and location to conduct remote vehicle appraisals.

TitleMax differentiates itself primarily through scale and accessibility: 900+ physical locations provide walk-in convenience, same-day funding is commonly available, and no traditional credit check is required since loans are secured by collateral. A mobile app (iOS and Android) and online customer portal at account.titlemax.com allow borrowers to check balances, view payment history, track due dates, and make payments remotely. These operational features are relatively polished for a subprime lender in this space.

However, TitleMax carries substantial documented risks for consumers. APRs routinely range from 100% to over 300%, with the Texas average reported at approximately 229%. The company has been cited by the CFPB twice — once in 2016 and again in 2023, when it was ordered to pay a $10 million civil penalty plus $5 million in consumer relief for violating the Military Lending Act (charging active-duty servicemembers roughly three times the federally mandated 36% APR cap), deliberately altering borrowers' records to conceal protected military status, and charging approximately 15,000 borrowers for insurance that provided no actual coverage. A class action lawsuit for additional MLA violations was filed in 2024. TitleMax is classified by the CFPB as a repeat offender. These facts make it unsuitable for military families and a high-risk choice for anyone who cannot confidently repay within the loan term.

pricing.guarantee_details: No money-back guarantee offered. Title loans are short-term debt instruments; terms and fees are disclosed at application and vary by state.

pros: One of the largest title lenders in the US with 900+ physical store locations across 14+ states
No traditional credit check required — loan approval based on vehicle equity, not credit score
Borrower keeps driving their vehicle throughout the entire loan repayment period
Same-day funding available at in-store locations
Remote vehicle appraisal via the proprietary Fast Track Appraiser™ smartphone app eliminates dealership visits
Mobile app and online portal (account.titlemax.com) support balance checks, payment history, and payments
25+ years of operating history since 1998 with established state lending licenses
cons: APRs commonly range from 100% to 300%+, with Texas average reported at approximately 229% — among the highest-cost consumer credit products available
CFPB issued a $10 million civil penalty in 2023 for Military Lending Act violations and charging ~15,000 borrowers for fictitious insurance coverage
Classified as a repeat CFPB offender following enforcement actions in both 2016 and 2023
Active-duty military servicemembers are effectively prohibited from using TitleMax due to federal MLA restrictions the company has repeatedly violated
Vehicle repossession is a direct consequence of non-repayment — borrowers risk losing their primary transportation
best_for: Consumers with poor or no credit history who own a vehicle outright and need fast cash
Borrowers who have been declined by banks and credit unions and need same-day funds against vehicle equity
People in TitleMax's 14+ operating states who prefer an in-person lending experience at a physical branch
Short-term borrowers who are confident they can repay quickly to minimize extremely high interest costs
diagnosis: TitleMax is best suited for credit-challenged consumers who own their vehicle outright and need rapid access to cash with no credit score requirement. The primary caveat is severe: APRs averaging 229% in Texas and documented federal violations — including a $10 million CFPB penalty for targeting military families — make TitleMax a high-risk, last-resort option that should be approached only when all lower-cost alternatives have been exhausted.
services: Car title loans (secured by vehicle title, up to ~$10,000)
Unsecured personal loans up to $2,500 (AZ, SC, TX)
Online loan applications via Fast Track Appraiser™ app
Remote vehicle appraisal by video (Fast Track Appraiser™)
Mobile app account management (iOS and Android)
Online customer portal for balance, payments, and payment history (account.titlemax.com)
In-store applications at 900+ branch locations across 14+ states
